stories.title: The Weaver's Bargain
stories.text:
Kael found the loom in a clearing where time moved sideways. Three threads hung suspended—past, present, future—and a woman with eyes like starlight sat at the bench, humming in a language older than kingdoms. "You've come to undo your oath," she said without looking up. It wasn't a question. Kael had sworn himself to the merchant lord Torvin for seven years in exchange for passage out of the Burned Expanse. But the oath was killing him—each broken promise pulled his shadow tighter until he could barely cast one. Now the fae-woman offered a choice: unravel the oath by reweaving the moment he swore it, or pay a different price. "What price?" Kael asked. "Stay here. Become my loom-keeper. The world will remember you kept your oath. But you will not." She smiled, terrible and kind. "You will be happy. Free. And utterly unknown."

Kael looked at the threads. In one, Torvin died without his service. In another, Kael withered, oath-bound and hollow.

stories.title: The Rust-Thief's Second Dance
stories.text:
Marin the Copper-Handed had stolen the Tear of Ashglass once. Stealing it twice should have been easier. It wasn't. The Sovereign's vault in Pelimor's Treasurer's Quarter had been reinforced since the last theft—new locks, new wards, new ledger-magic that screamed when touched. But Marin had something the vault-makers didn't: a half-brother who'd gone work as a scribe there, and a debt to settle. She met him in the Lantern Market at dusk. Brennan was pale, haunted. "The ledger-magic is alive," he whispered. "It remembers your touch. If you go in, it will *sing*."

"Then I don't go in," Marin said. "You do."

Brennan's eyes widened. "No—"

"You walk to the vault. You unlock it yourself. The magic knows your heartbeat; it won't scream." Marin pressed a copper ring into his palm. "You take the Tear. You leave. You get on a caravan south and never speak to me again."

"After which?" Brennan's voice cracked.

stories.title: The Deepkin Emissary's Mercy
stories.text:
The surface council met in the Stone Hall of Pelimor, arguing about the Tear's weakening. Commander Kesh arrived late, her deepkin armor leaving wet prints on the marble—a breach of protocol, and entirely deliberate. "The abyss does not wait for surface courtesy," she announced. "The Drowned God wakes hungry. The Tear cannot hold another season."

The Regent rose, pale. "Then the covenant fails. We prepared for this—"

"You prepared for war," Kesh said quietly. Kesh had seen what lay beneath the Sapphire Deep: consciousness-fragments drifting like kelp, singing in harmonies that predated language. The deepkin were fractured too, caught between ancient loyalty and survival. She'd watched her own reflection rebel against her commands. And she'd understood, finally, that the boundary was failing not from weakness but from competing hungers—two peoples so transformed by the Luminous Ascension that neither remembered how to coexist. "A willing sacrifice," Kesh said. "One of my commanders will take the Tear into the abyss and merge with it. The merged consciousness will *become* the new barrier. Not sealed. Alive.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Inheritance
stories.text: When Mesek died in the Drowned Cathedrals, he left Valern a gift wrapped in leather: a bone-flute carved in the Elder Tongue, singing even when silent. The Dominion wanted it destroyed. They sent auditors to every waystation, every refugee camp, searching for bone-carved artifacts. The pre-human language was becoming dangerous—each sung syllable rewrote a small corner of reality, and the Dominion's carefully maintained ledgers were beginning to contradict themselves. One elder bone-carver had taught seventeen transformed merchants the old words. Now those merchants hung suspended at the Drowned Cathedrals, changing and unchanging, caught between iterations. Valern was seventeen. She'd learned three songs from Mesek before he died. She knew the auditors would find her if she stayed in the refugee network. So she walked into the Sunken Reaches alone, carrying nothing but the flute, and found the hidden catacomb where the pre-human dead still taught. The bones there were older than kingdoms—jaw-strings of something vast, ribs like archways, a skull carved with the language that shaped water and shadow and time. She placed Mesek's flute among them.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Mercy-Kill
stories.text:
Tharen the Cartographer had mapped nine iterations of the world. Nine times the stars had died and been reborn. Nine times the lands had shifted, the peoples scattered, the coastlines redrawn. His consciousness was fragmentary now—pieces of him still lived in the old worlds, drawing maps on paper that no longer existed. Henryk found him in the Archive Tower's deepest vault, surrounded by maps that contradicted each other. Some showed the Ashen Reach as ocean. Others showed it as infinite mountain. A few showed neither—just the word *BETWEEN* repeated across the parchment. "We have to stop it," Henryk said. "The tenth Ascension is coming. If we can destroy enough maps—unwrite the cartography—we can prevent the Tower's completion."

Tharen laughed, a sound like paper tearing. "You cannot unwrite the world by destroying its pictures. The world writes itself now. The maps are just *descriptions* that lag behind."

He stood, and Henryk saw how fragile he was—held together by ink and the memory of boundaries. "But I can do something else. I can map what's coming.

stories.title: The Bone-Thief's Mercy
stories.text:
Corvhen the Bone-Thief knelt in the catacombs beneath Anthrim, her hands trembling as she carved the final glyph into the ancient skull. Seven years she had stolen bones from sacred tombs, binding them into her mother's animated form—a shambling shadow that wore her face but moved like pulled string. The Dominion's records called it resurrection. Her heart called it damnation. A footstep echoed through the stone. Meira, her old captain from the resistance, descended the steps with a torch. "The bones must be returned," Meira said quietly. "Or she will never rest."

Corvhen looked at her mother's hollow eyes—ancient, suffering, aware. The magic that bound her was eating her consciousness meal by meal. She had been paying the price in pieces of herself. "I know," Corvhen whispered. She raised the bone-knife—the same tool bone-singers used to teach the old language—and sang. Not a resurrection song, but a farewell. The skeleton collapsed into dust that remembered sunlight. Her mother's true death, at last. Meira knelt beside her.

stories.title: The Tidecaller's Daughter
stories.text: Sera of Verdant Strand inherited her father's charts but not his certainty. The Tidecaller's Key hung around her neck—a bronze compass that sang when the currents aligned—and every night it sang of a ship burning on a distant reef. Captain Vorn's vessel, the reports said. A merchant-prince's flagship lost to storm three days' sail northwest. The crew was drowning; the merchant's daughter was aboard. Sera's father had sworn an oath to the sea: never sail toward fire, never interrupt what the tides had chosen. Her mother was a widow because of that oath. But the Tidecaller's Key was singing louder, and Sera had inherited not chains but choice. She gathered a crew of fisher-folk and sailors with nothing to lose. The sea fought them—waves like walls, wind that screamed prophecy, currents that pulled backward. But Sera sang the old merchant-songs, and the water listened. They reached the reef at dawn. The merchant's daughter clung to a spar, half-drowned. The merchant prince himself had already sunk, weighted by his own ledgers.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Last Hunger
stories.text:
Skarath remembered when gold meant something. Three centuries of hoarding had taught her the weight of wealth—the smell of it, the warmth when she lay atop it in her mountain lair. But since the Luminous Ascension, the gold was cold. Useless. A child's pile of pebbles. A merchant caravan had stumbled into her cavern, desperate. They carried fragments of consciousness in leather pouches—the only currency that mattered now. They offered to trade them for her hoard. Skarath laughed, a sound like avalanche. "What would I do with scraps of dead thought?"

But one of the merchants—a young woman with starlight in her eyes—spoke: "I was alive once. Before the Ascension. I remember being alive. My memories are worth more than gold ever was."

Skarath descended from her useless mountain of metal and sat across from the woman. For the first time in centuries, the wyvern-lord listened. The merchant spoke of gardens, of rain, of a name carved into a stone that mattered more than empires. She described love in words that made Skarath's draconic heart ache.

stories.title: The Glasswright's Heist
stories.text: The Dominion's Glass-Tax Collectors had taken Corvin's workshop. The Hearthlands needed glass for its reborn ledgers—transparent vessels for prophecy-ink—and the Crown demanded quotas. Corvin's refusal to melt down heirloom glass (his grandmother's work, carrying the old songlines) had marked him as an obstructionist. He stole his own creations back. The Lampwick Quarter's vaults ran beneath the glass-makers' streets, three centuries of masterwork locked behind auditor-seals. Corvin knew every blind corner—he had walked them as an apprentice, learning which panes sang and which merely reflected. He moved through the darkness like water through his own medium. The theft took a single night. Twenty pieces of ancient glass, each one inscribed with pre-Ascension light-language that the Dominion's scholars couldn't read. The authorities would hunt him. But Corvin wasn't fleeing to the wilderness. He carried the glass to the Counter-Archive, where Sevin the Auditor was rebuilding what the Dominion had burned. Corvin laid each pane on the table, and they chimed—a frequency that hurt the ears of anyone trying to transcribe false records nearby.

stories.title: The Wastes-Singer's Song
stories.text:
Isara the Falconer had not left her eyrie in the Steppe Marches for three winters, but the eagle brought her news: a singing in the Bleached Wastes—old, pre-human, and growing louder. The Wanderer's questions were failing. Something beneath the earth was waking up and remembering how to speak. She took Ashira, her hawk-companion (a creature of the high thermals, ancient as the mountains), and rode east toward the Ashen Reach. The Wastes themselves seemed to resist her passage—heat shimmered in patterns too deliberate to be natural, and the dust beneath her horse's hooves hummed. At the ruins of a pre-human settlement, she found a bone-singer—a young woman named Valren, barely more than an initiate, standing with her hands pressed against a wall inscribed in the Elder Tongue. The wall was singing. Valren was learning. "The dead want to teach," Valren said without turning. "They say nine worlds have ended. This is the tenth. They say the Wanderer is trying to prevent what must happen."

Isara listened to the wall's song, and Ashira's feathers stood on end.
